skill.name: biopython-structure
skill.description: Use Bio.PDB to parse and analyze protein structures (PDB/mmCIF) for structural bioinformatics tasks; use when you need structure parsing, geometry calculations, or structural comparison/superposition.
skill.summary: Use Bio.PDB to parse and analyze protein structures (PDB/mmCIF) for structural bioinformatics tasks; use when you need structure parsing, geometry calculations, or structural comparison/superposition.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 131 static analysis findings are false positives with no malicious intent detected. The high-severity 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags match non-cryptographic identifiers in protein structure analysis code (no cryptographic operations exist). The medium-severity 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' flags are Markdown code fence formatting for Python documentation. The low-severity 'System reconnaissance' and 'Hidden file access' flags are standard Bio.PDB data access patterns. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malware was found.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm (False Positive - All 25 occurrences dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged non-cryptographic identifiers as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. No cryptographic operations (MD5, DES, AES, or any hashing) exist in the skill. All flagged locations contain standard protein structure data access code (e.g., residue IDs, chain selection, secondary structure codes). These are false positives.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance (False Positive - All 7 occurrences dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged protein structure identifier access (chain IDs, residue IDs, atom coordinates) as 'system reconnaissance'. These are standard Bio.PDB operations for accessing the SMCRA protein structure hierarchy, not system information gathering.

low_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution (False Positive - All ~103 occurrences dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged triple backtick Markdown code fences as Ruby/shell backtick command execution. All occurrences are Markdown formatting delimiters for Python code examples and documentation. No actual shell command execution, Ruby code, or command injection exists.

low_findings.title: Hidden file access (False Positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged .cif/.mmcif file extensions as 'hidden file access'. These are standard PDB-exchange file extensions for crystallographic structure files, not hidden dotfiles. No actual hidden file access occurs.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (False Positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a GitHub repository URL as a hardcoded network endpoint. This is a legitimate source attribution link to the skill's open-source repository. No data exfiltration or malicious endpoint exists.

content.value_statement: Protein structure analysis requires specialized bioinformatics tools that can parse complex PDB and mmCIF files. This skill provides ready-to-use guidance for Bio.PDB to compute geometry, find neighbors, and compare protein structures using standard structural bioinformatics methods.
actual_capabilities: Parse PDB and mmCIF structure files using Bio.PDB parsers with the SMCRA hierarchy
Calculate geometric measurements including distances, bond angles, and dihedral angles
Perform neighbor searches with spatial indexing for residue contact analysis
Superimpose protein structures and compute RMSD for structural comparison
Extract, modify, and save protein structures in PDB and mmCIF formats
Access secondary structure and solvent accessibility data through DSSP integration
limitations: Requires local PDB or mmCIF files; cannot fetch structures from the PDB database without additional scripting
DSSP secondary structure assignment requires an external mkdssp executable installed separately
Performance may degrade with very large macromolecular complexes or multimeric assemblies
Cannot visualize or render 3D protein structures; only provides coordinate-based analysis

use_cases.title: Analyze protein-ligand binding sites
use_cases.description: A structural biologist can parse a protein-ligand complex structure and use neighbor search to identify all residues within a cutoff distance of the ligand, enabling binding site characterization.

use_cases.title: Compare homologous protein structures
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics researcher can superimpose two related protein structures and compute RMSD to quantify structural similarity between wild-type and mutant variants.

use_cases.title: Validate protein structure quality
use_cases.description: A computational chemist can extract phi/psi angles for Ramachandran plot analysis and check for missing atoms to assess the quality of a predicted or solved protein structure.

prompt_templates.title: Parse and explore a PDB structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the biopython-structure skill to parse a PDB file at path 'data/1ubq.pdb'. List all chains in the first model and count the residues in each chain.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user has a PDB file and wants to understand its contents at the chain and residue level.

prompt_templates.title: Calculate distance between specific atoms
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the biopython-structure skill, parse 'data/1ubq.pdb' and calculate the distance between the CA atom of residue 10 in chain A and the CA atom of residue 45 in chain A. Report the distance in Angstroms.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs to measure the distance between two specific residues in a protein structure.

prompt_templates.title: Superimpose two structures and compute RMSD
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the biopython-structure skill, parse 'reference.pdb' and 'mobile.pdb'. Extract CA atoms from chain A of both structures and perform superposition. Report the RMSD value and save the superimposed structure to 'superimposed.pdb'.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user wants to compare two protein structures by calculating their structural similarity.

prompt_templates.title: Build a neighbor search pipeline with config file
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the biopython-structure skill, create a config file at 'config/task_config.json' with input_path 'data/1ubq.pdb', chain_id 'A', atom_name 'CA', and distance_cutoff 8.0. Then write a script that loads the config, parses the structure, performs neighbor search, and saves results to 'outputs/neighbors.json'.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs to set up a reproducible neighbor search workflow using configuration files.

output_examples.input: Parse the PDB file '1crn.pdb' and show the SMCRA hierarchy summary.
output_examples.output:
Structure ID: 1crn
Number of models: 1
Model 0:
  Chain A: 46 residues, 327 atoms
  Chain B: 46 residues, 327 atoms

output_examples.input: Find all residues within 5 Angstroms of the CA atom of residue 10 in chain A of '1ubq.pdb'.
output_examples.output:
Neighbor search results (cutoff: 5.0 Angstroms, level: residues):
Found 8 neighboring residues:
- Chain A, Residue 8 (ALA)
- Chain A, Residue 9 (LYS)
- Chain A, Residue 10 (ILE) - reference
- Chain A, Residue 11 (GLY)
- Chain A, Residue 12 (THR)
- Chain A, Residue 13 (GLU)
- Chain A, Residue 14 (PRO)
- Chain A, Residue 44 (ARG)

best_practices: Use mmCIF format for large structures and modern data, as it handles larger macromolecular complexes better than legacy PDB format
Set QUIET=True in PDBParser and MMCIFParser to suppress warnings from non-standard PDB files and focus on analysis results
Validate for missing atoms before performing geometric analysis to avoid incorrect distance and angle calculations
anti_patterns: Skipping sequence alignment before superposition leads to incorrect RMSD values when residue ordering differs between structures
Ignoring heteroatom residue IDs when iterating through structures causes errors since heteroatoms use tuple IDs with non-empty hetflag fields
Hard-coding file paths in scripts instead of using config files reduces reproducibility and makes workflows harder to adapt

faq.question: What file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports PDB format (.pdb) and mmCIF format (.cif, .mmcif). mmCIF is preferred for large structures.

faq.question: What software dependencies are required?
faq.answer: You need biopython version 1.79 or newer and numpy version 1.21 or newer. DSSP integration is optional and requires the mkdssp executable.

faq.question: How do I get PDB structure files to process?
faq.answer: This skill works with local files. You can download structures from the RCSB PDB database using tools like Bio.PDB.PDBList or by visiting the RCSB website directly.

faq.question: How does the SMCRA hierarchy work?
faq.answer: SMCRA stands for Structure, Model, Chain, Residue, Atom. A Structure contains one or more Models, each Model contains Chains, each Chain contains Residues, and each Residue contains Atoms.

faq.question: How do I calculate RMSD between two structures?
faq.answer: Use Bio.PDB.Superimposer. Parse both structures, extract corresponding atoms (typically CA atoms), call set_atoms followed by apply, and check the rms property. Align sequences first when residue order differs.

faq.question: Can this skill handle NMR structures with multiple models?
faq.answer: Yes. NMR structures often have multiple models. Use structure.get_models() to iterate through all models, or access a specific model by index with structure[model_index].
